items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.q: What is a balanced antecedent-consequent phrase?
items.correct: Two phrases of equal length where the first sounds open (question) and the second resolves (answer).
items.misconception: Any two phrases of similar style.
items.random_wrong: A long melody with no breaks.
items.why: Students miss the FUNCTIONAL relationship. The antecedent typically ends on an imperfect cadence (open); the consequent answers with a perfect cadence (closed). Length matches; cadences contrast.

items.q: A dot after a note adds?
items.why: It's A half of its value. “A quarter of its value” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many quavers equal a crotchet?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 quavers. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
